title: Deep Networks from the Principle of Rate Reduction

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DEEP NETWORKS FROM THE PRINCIPLE OF RATE REDUCTION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

This work attempts to interpret modern deep (convolutional) networks from the principles of rate reduction and (shift) invariant classification. We show that the basic iterative gradient ascent scheme for maximizing the rate reduction of learned features naturally leads to a deep network, one iteration per layer. The architectures, operators (linear or nonlinear), and parameters of the network are all explicitly constructed layer-by-layer in a forward propagation fashion. All components of this “white box” network have precise optimization, statistical, and geometric interpretation. Our preliminary experiments indicate that such a network can already learn a good discriminative deep representation without any back propagation training. Moreover, all linear operators of the so-derived network naturally become multi-channel convolutions when we enforce classification to be rigorously shift-invariant. The derivation also indicates that such a convolutional network is significantly more efficient to learn and construct in the spectral domain.

## 1 INTRODUCTION AND MOTIVATION

In recent years, various deep (convolution) network architectures such as AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012), VGG (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), ResNet (He et al., 2016), DenseNet (Huang et al., 2017), Recurrent CNN, LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997), Capsule Networks (Hinton et al., 2011), etc., have demonstrated very good performance in classification tasks of real-world datasets such as speeches or images. Nevertheless, almost all such networks are developed through years of empirical _trial and error_ , including both their architectures/operators and the ways they are to be effectively trained. Some recent practices even take to the extreme by searching for effective network structures and training strategies through extensive random search techniques, such as Neural Architecture Search (Zoph & Le, 2017; Baker et al., 2017), AutoML (Hutter et al., 2019), and Learning to Learn (Andrychowicz et al., 2016).

Despite tremendous empirical advances, there is still a lack of rigorous theoretical justification of the need or reasons for “deep” network architectures and a lack of fundamental understanding of the associated operators (e.g. multi-channel convolution and nonlinear activation) in each layer. As a result, deep networks are often designed and trained heuristically and then used as a “black box.” There have been a severe lack of guiding principles for each of the stages: For a given task, how wide or deep the network should be? What are the roles and relationships among the multiple (convolution) channels? Which parts of the networks need to be learned and trained and which can be determined in advance? How to evaluate the optimality of the resulting network? As a consequence, besides empirical evaluation, it is usually impossible to offer any rigorous guarantees for certain performance of a trained network, such as invariance to transformation (Azulay & Weiss, 2018; Engstrom et al., 2017) or overfitting noisy or even arbitrary labels (Zhang et al., 2017).

In this paper, we do not intend to address all these questions but we would attempt to offer a plausible interpretation of deep (convolution) neural networks by deriving a class of deep networks _from first principles_ . We contend that all key features and structures of modern deep (convolution) neural networks can be naturally derived from optimizing a principled objective, namely the _rate reduction_ recently proposed by Yu et al. (2020), that seeks a compact discriminative (invariant) representation of the data. More specifically, the basic iterative _gradient ascent_ scheme for optimizing the objective naturally takes the form of a deep neural network, one layer per iteration.

This principled approach brings a couple of nice surprises: First, architectures, operators, and parameters of the network can be constructed explicitly layer-by-layer in a _forward propagation_ fashion, and all inherit precise optimization, statistical and geometric interpretation. As result, the so constructed “white box” deep network already gives a good discriminative representation (and achieves good classification performance) _without any back propagation_ for training the deep network. Second, in the case of seeking a representation _rigorously_ invariant to shift or translation, the network naturally lends itself to a multi-channel convolutional network. Moreover, the derivation indicates such a convolutional network is computationally more efficient to learn and construct in the _spectral_

2 TECHNICAL APPROACH

Consider a basic classification task: given a set of _m_ samples _**X**_ = _[.]_ [ _**x**_[1] _, . . . ,_ _**x**[m]_ ] _∈_ R _[n][×][m]_ and their associated memberships _**π**_ ( _**x**[i]_ ) _∈_ [ _k_ ] in _k_ different classes, a deep network is typically used to model a direct mapping from the input data _**x** ∈_ R _[n]_ to its class label _f_ ( _**x** ,_ _**θ**_ ) : _**x** �→_ _**y** ∈_ R _[k]_ , where _**y**_ is typically a “one-hot” vector encoding the membership information _**π**_ ( _**x**_ ): the _j_ -th entry of _**y**_ is 1 iff _**π**_ ( _**x**_ ) = _j_ . The parameters _**θ**_ of the network is typically learned to minimize certain prediction loss, say the cross entropy loss, via gradient-descent type back propagation. Although this popular approach provides people a direct and effective way to train a network that predicts the class information, the so learned representation is however implicit and lacks clear interpretation.

## 2.1 PRINCIPLE OF RATE REDUCTION AND GROUP INVARIANCE

**The Principle of Maximal Coding Rate Reduction.** To help better understand features learned in a deep network, the recent work of Yu et al. (2020) has argued that the goal of (deep) learning is to learn a compact discriminative and diverse feature representation[1] _**z**_ = _f_ ( _**x**_ ) _∈_ R _[n]_ of the data _**x** f_ ( _**x**_ ) _h_ ( _**z**_ ) before any subsequent tasks such as classification: _**x** −−−→_ _**z** −−−→_ _**y** ._ To be more precise, instead of directly fitting the class label _**y**_ , a principled objective is to learn a feature map _f_ ( _**x**_ ) : _**x** �→_ _**z**_ which transforms the data _**x**_ onto a set of most discriminative low-dimensional linear subspaces _{S[j] }[k] j_ =1 _[⊂]_[R] _[n]_[, one subspace] _[ S][j]_[per class] _[ j][∈]_[[] _[k]_[]][.]

Let _**Z**_ = _[.]_ [ _**z**_[1] _, . . . ,_ _**z**[m]_ ] = [ _f_ ( _**x**_[1] ) _, . . . , f_ ( _**x**[m]_ )] be the features of the given samples _**X**_ . WLOG, we may assume all features _**z**[i]_ are normalized to be of unit norm: _**z**[i] ∈_ S _[n][−]_[1] . For convenience, let **Π** _[j] ∈_ R _[m][×][m]_ be a diagonal matrix whose diagonal entries encode the membership of samples/features belong to the _j_ -th class: **Π** _[j]_ ( _i, i_ ) = _**π**_ ( _**x**[i]_ ) = _**π**_ ( _**z**[i]_ ). Then based on principles from lossy data compression (Ma et al., 2007), Yu et al. (2020) has suggested that the optimal representation _**Z** ⋆ ⊂_ S _[n][−]_[1] should maximize the following coding rate reduction objective, known as the MCR[2] principle:

where _α_ = _n/_ ( _mϵ_[2] ), _αj_ = _n/_ (tr( **Π** _[j]_ ) _ϵ_[2] ), _γj_ = tr( **Π** _[j]_ ) _/m_ for _j_ = 1 _, . . . , k_ . Given a prescribed quantization error _ϵ_ , the first term _R_ of ∆ _R_ ( _**Z**_ ) measures the total coding length for all the features _**Z**_ and the second term _Rc_ is the sum of coding lengths for features in each of the _k_ classes.

In Yu et al. (2020), the authors have shown the optimal representation _**Z** ⋆_ that maximizes the above object indeed has desirable nice properties. Nevertheless, they adopted a conventional deep network (e.g. the ResNet) as a black box to model and parameterize the feature mapping: _**z**_ = _f_ ( _**x** ,_ _**θ**_ ). It has empirically shown that with such a choice, one can effectively optimize the MCR[2] objective and obtain discriminative and diverse representations for classifying real image data sets.

However, there remain several unanswered problems. Although the resulting feature representation is more interpretable, the network itself is still not. It is _not_ clear why any chosen network is able to optimize the desired MCR[2] objective: Would there be any potential limitations? The good empirical results (say with a ResNet) do not necessarily justify the particular choice in architectures and operators of the network: Why is a layered model necessary, how wide and deep is adequate, and is there any rigorous justification for the use of convolutions and nonlinear operators used? In Section 2.2, we show that using gradient ascent to maximize the rate reduction ∆ _R_ ( _**Z**_ ) naturally leads to a “white box” deep network that represents such a mapping. All linear/nonlinear operators and parameters of the network are _explicitly constructed in a purely forward propagation fashion_ . **Group Invariant Rate Reduction.** So far, we have considered the data and features as vectors. In many applications, such as serial data or imagery data, the semantic meaning (labels) of the data and their features are _invariant_ to certain transformations g _∈_ G (for some group G) (Cohen & Welling, 2016). For example, the meaning of an audio signal is invariant to shift in time; and the identity of an object in an image is invariant to translation in the image plane. Hence, we prefer the feature mapping _f_ ( _**x** ,_ _**θ**_ ) is rigorously invariant to such transformations:

The recent work of Zaheer et al. (2017); Maron et al. (2020) characterize properties of networks and operators for set permutation groups. Nevertheless, it remains challenging to learn features via a deep network that are _guaranteed_ to be invariant even to simple transformations such as translation and rotation (Azulay & Weiss, 2018; Engstrom et al., 2017). In Section 2.3, we show that the MCR[2] principle is compatible with invariance in a very natural and precise way: we only need to assign all transformed versions _{_ _**x** ◦_ g _|_ g _∈_ G _}_ into the same class as _**x**_ and map them all to the same subspace _S_ .[2] We will rigorously show (in the Appendices) that, when the group G is (discrete) circular 1D shifting or 2D translation, the resulting deep network naturally becomes a _multi-channel convolution network_ !

Notice that all parameters of the network are explicitly constructed layer by layer in a _forward propagation_ fashion. Once constructed, there is no need of any additional supervised learning, say via back propagation. As suggested in Yu et al. (2020), the so learned features can be directly used for classification via a nearest subspace classifier.

**Comparison with Other Approaches and Architectures.** Structural similarities between deep networks and iterative optimization schemes, especially those for solving sparse coding, have been long noticed. In particular, Gregor & LeCun (2010) has argued that algorithms for sparse coding, such as the FISTA algorithm (Beck & Teboulle, 2009), can be viewed as a deep network and be trained for better coding performance, known as LISTA. Later Monga et al. (2019); Sun et al. (2020) have proposed similar interpretation of deep networks as unrolling algorithms for sparse coding. Like all networks that are inspired by unfolding certain iterative optimization schemes, the structure of the ReduNet naturally contains a skip connection between adjacent layers as in the ResNet (He

But a major difference here is that all components (layers, channels, and operators) of the ReduNet are by explicit construction from first principles and they all have precise optimization, statistical and geometric interpretation. Furthermore, there is no need to learn them from back-propagation, although in principle one still could if further fine-tuning of the network is needed (see Remark 3 of Appendix A for more discussions).

## 2.3 DEEP CONVOLUTION NETWORKS FROM SHIFT-INVARIANT RATE REDUCTION

We next examine ReduNet from the perspective of invariance to transformation. Using the basic and important case of shift/translation invariance as an example, we will show that for data which are compatible with an invariant classifier, the ReduNet construction automatically takes the form of _a (multi-channel) convolutional neural network_ , rather than heuristically imposed upon.

**1D Serial Data and Shift Invariance.** For one-dimensional data _**x** ∈_ R _[n]_ under shift symmetry, we take G to be the group of circular shifts. Each observation _**x**[i]_ generates a family _{_ _**x**[i] ◦_ g _|_ g _∈_ G _}_ of shifted copies, which are the columns of the circulant matrix circ( _**x**[i]_ ) _∈_ R _[n][×][n]_ (see Appendix B.1 or Kra & Simanca (2012) for properties of circulant matrices).
What happens if we construct the ReduNet from these families _**Z**_ 1 = [circ( _**x**_[1] ) _, . . . ,_ circ( _**x**[m]_ )]? The data covariance matrix:

associated with this family of samples is _automatically_ a (symmetric) circulant matrix. Moreover, because the circulant property is preserved under sums, inverses, and products, the matrices _**E**_ 1 and _**C**_ 1 _[j]_[are][also][automatically][circulant][matrices,][whose][application][to][a][feature][vector] _**[z]**_[can][be] implemented using cyclic convolution “⊛” (see Proposition B.1 of Appendix B):

Because _g_ ( _·,_ _**θ**_ 1) consists only of operations that co-vary with cyclic shifts, the features _**Z**_ 2 at the next level again consist of families of shifts: _**Z**_ 2 = �circ( _**x**_[1] + _ηg_ ( _**x**_[1] _,_ _**θ**_ 1)) _, . . . ,_ circ( _**x**[m]_ + _ηg_ ( _**x**[m] ,_ _**θ** m_ ))� _._ Continuing inductively, we see that all matrices _**E** ℓ_ and _**C** ℓ[j]_[based on such] _**[ Z]**[ℓ]_[are circulant. By virtue] of the properties of the data, ReduNet has taken the form of a convolutional network, _with no need to explicitly choose this structure!_

**The Role of Multiple Channels and Sparsity.** There is one problem though: In general, the set of all circular permutations of a vector _**z**_ give a full-rank matrix. That is, the _n_ “augmented” features associated with each sample (hence each class) typically already span the entire space R _[n]_ . The MCR[2] objective (1) will not be able to distinguish classes as different subspaces.

One natural remedy is to improve the separability of the data by “lifting” the features to a higher dimensional space, e.g., by taking their responses to multiple, filters _**k**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**k** C ∈_ R _[n]_ :

The filers can be pre-designed invariance-promoting filters,[5] or adaptively learned from the data,[6] or randomly selected as we do in our experiments. However, this operation does not yet render the classes separable – features associated with other classes will span the _same n_ -dimensional subspace. This reflects a fundamental conflict between linear (subspace) modeling and invariance. One way of resolving this conflict is to leverage additional structure within each class, in the form of _sparsity_ : signals within each class can be assumed to be generated not as arbitrary linear combinations of basis vectors, but as sparse combinations of atoms of different (incoherent) dictionaries _Dj_ . Under this assumption, if the convolution kernels _{_ _**k** c}_ match well the sparsifying dictionaries,[7] the multi-channel responses should be sparse. Hence we may take an entry-wise _sparsity-promoting nonlinear thresholding_ , say _**τ**_ ( _·_ ), on the filter outputs by setting small (say absolute value below _ϵ_ ) or negative responses to be zero:[8]
These features can be assumed to lie on a lower-dimensional (nonlinear) submanifold of R _[n][×][C]_ , which can be linearized and separated from the other classes by subsequent ReduNet layers.

This multi-channel ReduNet retains the good invariance properties described above: all _**E** ℓ_ and _**C** ℓ[j]_ matrices are block circulant, and represent _multi-channel 1D circular convolutions_ (see Proposition B.2 of Appendix B for a rigorous statement and proof):

Hence by construction, the resulting ReduNet is a deep convolutional network for multi-channel 1D signals. Unlike Xception nets Chollet (2017), these multi-channel convolutions in general are _not_ depthwise separable.[9]

**Connections to Recurrent and Convolutional Sparse Coding.** The sparse coding perspective of Gregor & LeCun (2010) is later extended to recurrent and convolutional networks for serial data, e.g. Wisdom et al. (2016); Papyan et al. (2016); Sulam et al. (2018); Monga et al. (2019). Although both sparsity and convolution are advocated as desired characteristics for deep networks, they do not explicitly justify the necessity of sparsity and convolutions from the objective of the network, say classification. In our framework, we see how multi-channel convolutions ( _**E**_[¯] _,_ _**C**_[¯] _[j]_ ), different � nonlinear activations ( _**π**[j] ,_ _**τ**_ ), and the sparsity requirement are derived _from_ , rather than heuristically proposed for, the objective of maximizing rate reduction of the features while enforcing shift invariance.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

We now _verify_ whether the so constructed ReduNet achieves its design objectives through experiments on synthetic data and real images. The datasets and experiments are chosen to clearly demonstrate the behaviors of the network obtained by our algorithm, in terms of learning the correct discriminative representation and truly achieving invariance. It is not the purpose of this work to push the state of the art on any real datasets with highly engineered networks and systems, although we believe this framework has this potential in the future. All code is implemented in Python mainly using NumPy. All our experiments are conducted in a computer with 2.8 GHz Intel i7 CPU and 16GB of memory. 4 CONCLUSIONS AND FUTURE WORK

This work offers an interpretation of deep (convolutional) networks by construction from first principles. It provides a rigorous explanation for the deep architecture and components from the perspective of optimizing the rate reduction objective. Simulations and experiments on basic data sets clearly verify the so-constructed ReduNet achieves the desired functionality and objective. Although in this work the ReduNet is forward constructed, one may study how to effectively fine tune it via back propagation. We believe rate reduction provides a principled framework for designing new networks with interpretable architectures and operators that can scale up to real-world datasets and problems, with better performance guarantees. This framework can also be naturally extended to settings of _online_ or _unsupervised_ learning if **Π** is partially or not known and is to be optimized.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a compelling and ambitious attempt to derive deep convolutional networks from first principles using the rate reduction objective and shift-invariant classification. It offers a white-box construction of network architectures, operators, and parameters with clear optimization and geometric interpretations. The derivation of multi-channel convolutions from invariance principles is particularly elegant, and the spectral domain efficiency argument adds practical relevance. The work stands out for its theoretical coherence and potential to provide interpretability in deep learning design.

Overview issues:

- Rate reduction objective lacks formal justification for discriminative power: The paper assumes that maximizing rate reduction alone leads to discriminative feature representations, but this claim is not rigorously justified. Equation (1) defines a coding rate reduction objective, yet the connection between this information-theoretic measure and classification accuracy is not established. The experiments show features become orthogonal across classes, but orthogonality does not guarantee good classification performance—especially when classes are not linearly separable or have complex decision boundaries. The paper should either provide theoretical justification for why maximizing ΔR ensures class separability, or include classification accuracy metrics on standard benchmarks to empirically validate discriminative quality. Without this, the core claim that rate reduction yields good representations remains speculative.

- Shift invariance construction assumes perfect circular symmetry incompatible with real data: The derivation of convolutional structure in Section 2.3 relies on modeling data as invariant under circular shifts, leading to circulant covariance matrices and hence convolution operators. However, real-world images and signals are not circularly shifted—they have boundaries and asymmetric content. The assumption that all shifted versions of a sample (including wrap-around shifts) belong to the same class is unrealistic. For example, shifting an image so that most of it wraps to the opposite side creates a nonsensical pattern that should not be classified the same way. This undermines the practical relevance of the invariance argument. The paper should either relax this assumption using more realistic group actions or demonstrate that approximate shift invariance still leads to convolution-like operators in practice.

- Layer-wise construction ignores feature distribution shift and error accumulation: The forward propagation construction of ReduNet layers assumes that each layer's parameters can be computed independently based on the current feature statistics. However, this ignores the fact that early layers may produce poor features due to insufficient separation, which then propagate and compound in subsequent layers. The method provides no mechanism for correcting earlier decisions—once a layer is constructed, it is fixed. This contrasts sharply with backpropagation-based training, which allows later layers to influence earlier ones. The paper should address how the network avoids getting stuck in poor representations due to early mistakes, perhaps by analyzing convergence properties or providing stability guarantees across layers.

- Sparsity assumption introduced ad hoc without theoretical or empirical support: In Section 2.3, the paper identifies a fundamental conflict between linear subspace modeling and invariance, then proposes sparsity as a solution. However, the introduction of sparsity via thresholding (Eq. 15) appears abruptly, without justification from the rate reduction principle. There is no explanation of how sparsity emerges from or optimizes ΔR, nor is there any analysis of when or why this nonlinear operation helps. The experiments do not ablate the effect of sparsity, so its necessity is unverified. If sparsity is essential to the framework, it should be derived from the objective; otherwise, the paper overstates the completeness of its first-principles derivation.

- Evaluation lacks comparison to baseline methods and real-world benchmarks: The experiments focus on synthetic data (Gaussians, sinusoidal signals) and toy MNIST subsets, showing that features become orthogonal and invariant. However, there is no comparison to standard CNNs, autoencoders, or other representation learning methods trained without backpropagation (e.g., PCANet, scattering networks). Nor are standard metrics like classification accuracy, robustness to perturbations, or transfer performance reported. Without such comparisons, it is impossible to assess whether ReduNet learns better representations than existing approaches. The claim that it 'learns a good discriminative deep representation' remains unsubstantiated for practical applications.

- Parameter construction depends on class membership during training: The derivation of the increment function g(z,θ) in Eq. (8) relies on class membership indicators π^j(z), which are estimated using the softmax of filter responses. However, this creates a circular dependency: the filters C_ℓ^j depend on class assignments, but the class assignments depend on the filters. The paper sidesteps this by using ground-truth labels to compute C_ℓ^j, but this limits the method to fully supervised settings. In contrast, most self-supervised or unsupervised representation learning methods do not require labels. The paper should clarify whether ReduNet can operate without labels, or acknowledge that its 'white-box' construction still depends on supervision, undermining claims of being a fully principled alternative to backpropagation.

- No demonstration that rate reduction separates nonlinearly separable classes: The paper claims the ReduNet learns discriminative representations by maximizing rate reduction, but only evaluates performance on linearly separable or trivially clustered data (e.g., Gaussians on spheres). It does not test whether the method can separate classes with complex, nonlinear decision boundaries—such as two concentric circles or interlaced manifolds—where rate reduction might fail to induce meaningful separation. This matters because real-world data often require nonlinear separation. To validate the objective’s strength, the authors should construct a 2D synthetic dataset with two classes arranged in non-convex, interwoven patterns (e.g., Swiss roll halves), apply ReduNet, and visualize if the learned features become linearly separable in the final layer. Without this, the claim that rate reduction yields good representations lacks bite.

- Missing concrete parametric example of ReduNet construction: The paper derives ReduNet layer-by-layer but does not compute its operators for any fully specified model. A natural check is to instantiate the construction on a simple, analytically tractable case—such as a mixture of two spherical Gaussians in 2D with known means and variances—and explicitly compute the first-layer matrices $E_1$ and $C_1^j$ from training samples. This would let readers verify that the expansion and compression operators behave as claimed: $E_1$ spreads features uniformly, while $C_1^j$ compresses each class toward its mean. Skipping this step leaves the construction abstract and uncalibrated. The authors should add such a computation to ground the theory in a tangible setting.

- No end-to-end implementation guidance for high-dimensional data: While the paper emphasizes computational efficiency in the spectral domain, it provides no practical details on scaling ReduNet to high-dimensional inputs like images. For instance, computing $(I + \alpha Z Z^*)^{-1}$ in Eq. (4) becomes infeasible when $n$ is large, yet the paper does not specify how to approximate this inversion or manage memory. Standard practice would involve structured approximations or randomized linear algebra. The authors should describe how to implement the $E_\ell$ and $C_\ell^j$ operators efficiently for $n=784$ (MNIST) using, say, low-rank approximations or FFT-based convolution, and report runtime or complexity per layer. Without this, the method’s scalability remains speculative.

- Failure to recover standard CNNs as a limiting case: The paper claims ReduNet naturally becomes a convolutional network under shift invariance, but does not show that this construction recovers known CNN architectures in the limit. A strong validation would be to demonstrate that when applied to high-frequency sinusoidal inputs, the derived filters $\bar{e}, \bar{c}^j$ converge to Gabor-like kernels resembling those learned in early CNN layers. The authors should compute these filters explicitly from Eq. (16) on a synthetic dataset of shifted sinusoids and plot their spatial profiles. If they resemble standard edge detectors, it strengthens the claim; if not, the connection to practice weakens.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide theoretical justification or empirical validation linking the rate reduction objective to classification performance.

- Revise the shift invariance argument to account for non-circular, realistic data transformations or demonstrate robustness to boundary effects.

- Analyze the stability and error propagation of the layer-wise construction, possibly with ablation studies or convergence proofs.

- Either derive sparsity from the rate reduction principle or remove it as a core component of the framework.

- Include comparisons to baseline representation learning methods on standard benchmarks with classification accuracy and robustness metrics.

comments.title: Spectral efficiency claim lacks supporting assumptions
comments.quote: Hence, to compute  $\bar{\pmb{E}}$  and  $\bar{\pmb{C}}^j \in \mathbb{R}^{nC \times nC}$ , we only have to compute in the frequency domain the inverse of  $C \times C$  blocks for  $n$  times and the overall complexity would be  $O(nC^3)$  instead of  $O((nC)^3)$  for inverting a generic  $nC \times nC$  matrix.
comments.explanation: The claimed complexity reduction to $O(nC^3)$ assumes that the multi-channel feature process is jointly circulant across channels, so that the DFT diagonalizes the full covariance into $n$ independent $C \times C$ spectral blocks. However, this joint stationarity is not explicitly stated or justified in the main text. Without this assumption, the off-diagonal blocks in the spectral representation may not be diagonal, and the decoupling fails. The derivation in Appendix B.3 likely assumes this, but the main paper should clarify the required conditions for the efficiency gain to hold.

comments.title: Covariance matrix definition omits normalization and contains typo
comments.quote:
$\bm{\Sigma}$, for all its shifted versions are given as:

\[ \text{circ}(\bar{\bm{z}})\doteq\begin{bmatrix}\text{circ}(\bm{z}[1])\\
\vdots\\
\text{circ}(\bm{\hat{z}}[C])\end{bmatrix}\ \in\mathbb{R}^{nC\times n},\quad\bm{\Sigma}\doteq\begin{bmatrix}\text{circ}(\bm{z}[1])\\
\vdots\\
\text{circ}(\bm{\hat{z}}[C])\end{bmatrix}\left[\,\text{circ}(\bm{z}[1])^{*},\ldots,\text{circ}(\bm{z}[C])^{*}\,\right]\ \in\mathbb{R}^{nC\times nC}, \] (14)
comments.explanation: The definition of $\bm{\Sigma}$ lacks normalization by the number of shifts ($n$) and does not subtract the mean, making it a second-moment matrix rather than a proper covariance. This undermines the spectral decomposition in (17), which typically applies to centered data. Additionally, $\bm{\hat{z}}[C]$ appears to be a typo and should be $\bm{z}[C]$ for consistency. The authors may intend $\bm{\Sigma}$ as a Gram matrix, but calling it a 'data covariance matrix' is misleading without clarification.

comments.title: Sparsity thresholding breaks shift equivariance and circulant structure
comments.quote:
Hence we may take an entry-wise sparsity-promoting nonlinear thresholding, say  $\pmb{\tau}(\cdot)$ , on the filter outputs by setting small (say absolute value below  $\epsilon$ ) or negative responses to be zero:

$$
\bar {\boldsymbol {z}} = \boldsymbol {\tau} \left[ \operatorname {c i r c} \left(\boldsymbol {k} _ {1}\right) \boldsymbol {z}, \dots , \operatorname {c i r c} \left(\boldsymbol {k} _ {C}\right) \boldsymbol {z} \right] \quad \in \mathbb {R} ^ {n \times C}. \tag {15}
$$
comments.explanation: Applying entry-wise thresholding $\boldsymbol{\tau}(\cdot)$ to the filter outputs destroys shift equivariance, as the operation is nonlinear and spatially localized. This breaks the circulant structure required for the subsequent derivation of $\bar{\boldsymbol{E}}$ and $\bar{\boldsymbol{C}}^j$ as multi-channel convolutions in (16). The paper does not clarify whether thresholding is applied before or after forming the circulant matrix, but equation (15) suggests it acts on vectors, not circulant representations. Without a mechanism to preserve shift symmetry—such as applying thresholding in a shift-invariant manner—the invariance properties claimed in Proposition B.2 do not follow.

comments.title: Block circulant structure post-thresholding lacks justification
comments.quote:
This multi-channel ReduNet retains the good invariance properties described above: all  $\pmb{E}_{\ell}$  and  $C_{\ell}^{j}$  matrices are block circulant, and represent multi-channel 1D circular convolutions (see Proposition B.2 of Appendix B for a rigorous statement and proof):

$$
\bar {\boldsymbol {E}} (\bar {\boldsymbol {z}}) = \bar {\boldsymbol {e}} * \bar {\boldsymbol {z}}, \quad \bar {\boldsymbol {C}} ^ {j} (\bar {\boldsymbol {z}}) = \bar {\boldsymbol {c}} ^ {j} * \bar {\boldsymbol {z}} \quad \in \mathbb {R} ^ {n \times C}, \quad j = 1, \dots , k, \tag {16}
$$
comments.explanation: The claim that $\bar{\boldsymbol{E}}$ and $\bar{\boldsymbol{C}}^j$ remain block circulant after sparsity thresholding relies on the data covariance $\bm{\Sigma}$ being block circulant. However, thresholding introduces nonlinear, data-dependent sparsity patterns that generally break the joint circulant structure unless the sparsity itself is shift-invariant—which is not assumed or verified. The paper cites Proposition B.2, but that result likely assumes linear or circulant data generation. The nonlinearity disrupts the symmetry needed for block circulant operators, creating a gap between the derivation and the proposed architecture.

comments.title: Gradient update ignores tangent space projection on sphere
comments.quote: This scheme can be interpreted as how one should incrementally adjust locations of the current features  $Z_{\ell}$  in order for the resulting  $Z_{\ell + 1}$  to improve the rate reduction  $\Delta R(\mathbf{Z})$ . Simple calculation shows that the gradient  $\frac{\partial \Delta R}{\partial \mathbf{Z}}$  entails evaluating the following derivatives of the terms in (1):
comments.explanation: The gradient ascent in (3) updates features in the ambient space and then projects onto $\mathbb{S}^{n-1}$, but it does not project the gradient itself onto the tangent space of the sphere. On a Riemannian manifold, valid gradient flow requires projecting the Euclidean gradient onto the tangent space at each point. Without this, the update direction may include radial components that are eliminated by normalization, distorting the intended dynamics. The current derivation treats the projection as a post-hoc correction rather than part of the gradient computation, which undermines the geometric interpretation of the update as a proper projected gradient method.

comments.title: Nonlinearity $\sigma$ misrepresented as filter bank activation
comments.quote: which is denoted as a nonlinear operator  $\sigma(\cdot)$  on outputs of the feature  $z_{\ell}$  through  $k$  banks of filters:  $[C_{\ell}^{1}, \ldots, C_{\ell}^{k}]$ . Notice that the nonlinearity arises due to a "soft" assignment of class membership based on the feature responses from those filters.
comments.explanation: The operator $\sigma$ is not a standard pointwise nonlinearity applied to concatenated filter outputs. Instead, it denotes a weighted sum $\sum_j \gamma_j C_\ell^j z_\ell \widehat{\pi}^j(z_\ell)$, where the weights depend nonlinearly on $\|C_\ell^j z_\ell\|$ via softmax. This creates a class-conditional linear combination, not a conventional activation function. The text and Figure 1 suggest a typical CNN-style filter bank with post-concatenation nonlinearity, which misrepresents the actual computation. Readers may incorrectly assume a standard architecture when the mechanism is fundamentally different.

comments.title: Construction depends on ground-truth class labels
comments.quote: where  $\pi^j (z_\ell)\in [0,1]$  indicates the probability of  $z_{\ell}$  belonging to the  $j$  -th class.3 Notice that the increment depends on 1). A linear map represented by  $E_{\ell}$  that depends only on statistics of all features from the preceding layer; 2). A set of linear maps  $\{C_\ell^j\}_{j = 1}^k$  and memberships  $\{\pi^j (z_\ell)\}_{j = 1}^k$  of the features.
comments.explanation: The operators $C_\ell^j$ are computed using $\Pi^j$, which requires ground-truth class labels to form class-specific covariances. Yet $\widehat{\pi}^j(z_\ell)$ depends on $\|C_\ell^j z_\ell\|$, creating a circular dependency: the class assignment depends on filters built from class labels. This means the entire construction of $g(z_\ell, \theta_\ell)$ relies on supervision during training. The paper does not acknowledge this limitation, implying broader applicability than is supported. A clarification is needed that the method is currently restricted to fully supervised settings.

comments.title: Invertibility of circulant matrices not guaranteed
comments.quote: Moreover, because the circulant property is preserved under sums, inverses, and products, the matrices $\bm{E}_{1}$ and $\bm{C}_{1}^{j}$ are also automatically circulant matrices, whose application to a feature vector $\bm{z}$ can be implemented using cyclic convolution “$\circledast$” (see Proposition B.1 of Appendix B):
comments.explanation: The circulant property is preserved under inversion only if the matrix is invertible. A circulant matrix is diagonalized by the Fourier transform, so its inverse exists iff all DFT coefficients of the first row are nonzero. The data covariance $\bm{Z}_1\bm{Z}_1^*$ is a sum of PSD circulant matrices, but its rank depends on spectral coverage. If the union of supports of $\widehat{\bm{x}}^i$ does not span $\mathbb{C}^n$, the matrix is singular. For example, constant signals yield rank-1 circulants. The paper does not assume conditions (e.g., $m \geq n$, diverse spectra) to ensure invertibility, so $\bm{E}_1$ may not exist or preserve circulant structure.

comments.title: Inductive step for circulant structure lacks proof
comments.quote: Continuing inductively, we see that all matrices $\bm{E}_{\ell}$ and $\bm{C}_{\ell}^{j}$ based on such $\bm{Z}_{\ell}$ are circulant.
comments.explanation: The inductive argument assumes that if $\bm{Z}_\ell$ consists of circulant blocks, then $\bm{Z}_{\ell+1}$ will too. But this depends on $g(\cdot, \theta_\ell)$ preserving shift orbits, which requires both linear and nonlinear components to be shift-equivariant. As thresholding breaks equivariance, there is no guarantee that $\bm{Z}_{\ell+1}$ remains a union of circulant matrices. The paper provides no proof that the circulant structure propagates beyond the first layer. A counterexample: ReLU after channel concatenation can produce asymmetric activation patterns that destroy circulant symmetry.

comments.title: Full $g$ cannot be implemented as single cyclic convolution
comments.quote: whose application to a feature vector $\bm{z}$ can be implemented using cyclic convolution “$\circledast$” (see Proposition B.1 of Appendix B):
comments.explanation: While the linear operators $\bm{E}_1$ and $\bm{C}_1^j$ are circulant and thus equivalent to cyclic convolutions, the full increment function $g(\cdot, \theta_1)$ includes nonlinear operations like $\bm{\sigma}([\bm{c}_1^1 \circledast \bm{z}_1, \ldots])$. These act in the spatial domain and destroy the diagonal structure in frequency space. Therefore, $g$ is not itself a convolution and cannot be implemented as a single cyclic convolution. The claim conflates the circulant nature of linear components with the full update. In reality, $g$ is a cascade of convolutions and nonlinearities—a standard CNN layer—but this structure arises from shift-equivariant components, not from $g$ being a convolution.

comments.title: Ambiguity in applying $g$ to shift orbits
comments.quote: the features $\bm{Z}_{2}$ at the next level again consist of families of shifts: $\bm{Z}_{2}=\left[\text{circ}(\bm{x}^{1}+\eta g(\bm{x}^{1},\bm{\theta}_{1})),\ldots,\text{circ}(\bm{x}^{m}+\eta g(\bm{x}^{m},\bm{\theta}_{m}))\right]$.
comments.explanation: The expression defines $\bm{Z}_2$ using $g$ applied only to base signals $\bm{x}^i$, then regenerating their orbits. But $\bm{Z}_1$ contains all shifted copies, so $g$ should be applied consistently across the orbit. This is valid only if $g$ is shift-equivariant. The paper does not specify whether $g$ is applied to each shifted copy individually or only to base signals. If applied only to base signals, the resulting $\bm{Z}_2$ may not match the forward propagation of all shifted inputs, breaking the assumption of orbit closure. Clarification and proof of equivalence under equivariance are needed.

comments.title: Matrix inverses in gradient may not exist
comments.explanation: The expressions for $E_\ell$ and $C_\ell^j$ involve matrix inverses $(\boldsymbol{I} + \alpha \boldsymbol{Z}_\ell \boldsymbol{Z}_\ell^*)^{-1}$ and $(\boldsymbol{I} + \alpha_j \boldsymbol{Z}_\ell \boldsymbol{\Pi}^j \boldsymbol{Z}_\ell^*)^{-1}$, which require the arguments to be invertible. But $\boldsymbol{Z}_\ell \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ with $m > n$ implies $\boldsymbol{Z}_\ell \boldsymbol{Z}_\ell^*$ is rank-deficient, and class-specific matrices may be singular if class sizes are small. The paper does not assume $m \leq n$ or include regularization. Without a small $\epsilon \boldsymbol{I}$ term, the inverse may not exist, undermining the construction of $E_\ell$ and $C_\ell^j$.

comments.title: Normalization step lacks convergence analysis
comments.explanation: The normalization step $\mathcal{P}_{\mathbb{S}^{n-1}}$ ensures unit norm but may distort gradient dynamics. Repeated normalization can suppress signal growth or cause oscillations if $\eta$ is not scaled appropriately. The paper assumes convergence after $L$ iterations but provides no conditions on $\eta$, $\gamma_j$, or operator norms that ensure stable progression. For example, large $\eta$ could cause updates to be projected onto a small neighborhood, stalling learning. A discussion of normalization effects and step size bounds is needed to support the convergence claim.

comments.title: Claim of orthogonal clusters in $\mathbb{S}^2$ is geometrically impossible
comments.quote: 2d-2f). We can observe similar behavior as in  $\mathbb{S}^2$ , i.e., samples from the same class converge to one cluster and different clusters are orthogonal to each other. Moreo
comments.explanation: In $\mathbb{R}^3$, three clusters on $\mathbb{S}^2$ cannot be pairwise orthogonal—orthogonality requires dot product zero, which implies 90-degree angles. But three mutually orthogonal unit vectors span $\mathbb{R}^3$ (like axes), while the paper describes means uniformly placed on $\mathbb{S}^2$, which implies ~120-degree separation. True orthogonality is geometrically impossible. The intended meaning is likely good separation or high MCR$^2$, not literal orthogonality. This imprecision risks confusion about what the objective achieves in higher dimensions.

comments.title: Overstated experimental validation of functionality
comments.quote: Simulations and experiments on basic data sets clearly verify the so-constructed ReduNet achieves the desired functionality and objective.
comments.explanation: The claim that experiments 'clearly verify' functionality overstates the evidence. Results are limited to synthetic data and MNIST subsets, with no classification accuracy, robustness, or comparison to baselines. Feature orthogonality does not imply discriminative performance, especially for nonlinear manifolds. The MCR$^2$ objective encourages separation, but the paper does not link this directly to classification efficacy. The validation is preliminary, not conclusive.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of scalability and performance guarantees
comments.quote: We believe rate reduction provides a principled framework for designing new networks with interpretable architectures and operators that can scale up to real-world datasets and problems, with better performance guarantees.
comments.explanation: The assertion of scalability and better performance guarantees lacks support. No experiments are conducted on real-world datasets, and the method's reliance on class labels limits unsupervised applicability. There are no formal generalization bounds, robustness analyses, or stability proofs. The layer-wise construction ignores error accumulation. Without such foundations, claiming 'better performance guarantees' exceeds what is demonstrated or justified.

comments.title: Misleading implication about unsupervised extension
comments.quote: This framework can also be naturally extended to settings of online or unsupervised learning if  $\Pi$  is partially or not known and is to be optimized.
comments.explanation: The extension to unsupervised learning is not 'natural' given the current derivation. The construction depends on $\Pi^j$ to compute class-specific operators $C_\ell^j$ and estimate $\pi^j(z_\ell)$. Without labels, $\Pi$ cannot be formed, and no mechanism is proposed for discovering classes from rate reduction alone. The paper does not explore clustering or self-supervised variants. This extension requires significant additional development and should not be presented as straightforward.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a novel theoretical framework for deriving deep network architectures from first principles of rate reduction and shift invariance. The authors construct a 'white box' network (ReduNet) where each layer corresponds to one iteration of gradient ascent on the rate reduction objective. The paper provides a principled derivation of convolutional structures from shift invariance requirements and demonstrates the approach on synthetic and simple real datasets. While the theoretical contribution is significant and potentially groundbreaking, the paper has several important gaps in its empirical validation, scalability analysis, and completeness of the theoretical framework.

The paper makes a substantial theoretical contribution by providing a principled derivation of deep network architectures from information-theoretic first principles. The mathematical derivations connecting rate reduction maximization to network layer formation are elegant and provide new insights into why deep architectures work. The geometric and statistical interpretations of network components are valuable for the field's understanding of deep learning. However, the empirical validation is preliminary and limited to simple synthetic datasets and basic MNIST experiments, leaving questions about scalability and practical performance unanswered.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient empirical validation for claimed performance without backpropagation: The paper claims that ReduNet can learn good discriminative representations without backpropagation training, but the experiments only validate this on synthetic Gaussian mixtures and very simple MNIST digit classification (just digits 0 and 1). The experiments use 2,000-3,500 iterations/layers, which is computationally intensive, and there is no demonstration of scalability to standard benchmarks like CIFAR-10 or ImageNet. The paper acknowledges that parameters are 'amenable to fine-tuning via back propagation' but doesn't clarify whether the forward-only construction achieves competitive performance. This gap matters because the central claim of learning without backpropagation is revolutionary but lacks convincing evidence beyond toy problems.

- Unproven convergence guarantees for the iterative layer construction: The paper shows that gradient ascent on rate reduction naturally leads to a deep network structure, but provides no theoretical convergence analysis. The iterative construction in Equation (3) and the layer-by-layer formation in Equation (11) lack guarantees about whether they converge to global or even good local optima of the rate reduction objective. The paper doesn't analyze the optimization landscape or provide convergence rates. This is a significant omission because without convergence guarantees, the practical reliability of the forward construction method remains questionable, especially for complex real-world data distributions where the objective may have many local optima.

- Incomplete derivation of nonlinear activations and their role: While the paper derives linear operators (convolutions) from first principles, the derivation of nonlinear activations is heuristic. The softmax function in Equation (9) and the sparsity-promoting thresholding in Equation (15) are motivated rather than rigorously derived from the rate reduction objective. The paper doesn't explain why these specific nonlinearities are optimal or how they emerge from the optimization problem. This matters because nonlinear activations are critical components of deep networks, and their ad-hoc nature undermines the claim that all components have 'precise optimization interpretation.'

- Limited analysis of computational complexity and scalability: The paper mentions spectral domain computation reduces complexity from O((nC)^3) to O(nC^3) but doesn't provide a comprehensive scalability analysis. There's no discussion of how the method scales with dataset size, number of classes, or dimensionality of input data. The experiments use small datasets (m=500-2000 samples) and the computational requirements for large-scale problems remain unclear. This is important because practical adoption requires understanding whether the method can handle modern datasets with millions of samples and high-dimensional inputs like images.

- Overly restrictive assumptions about invariance and data structure: The derivation relies heavily on shift invariance and assumes data can be represented as sparse combinations of incoherent dictionaries. The paper doesn't address what happens when these assumptions don't hold, which is common in real-world vision tasks where objects have multiple symmetries and complex structures. The conflict between linear subspace modeling and invariance mentioned in Section 2.3 is resolved by imposing sparsity, but this solution may not generalize well. This limitation matters because it constrains the applicability of the framework to problems that satisfy these strong assumptions.

- Missing demonstration that rate reduction is non-vacuous: The paper claims that maximizing rate reduction leads to discriminative representations, but never demonstrates that this objective is non-vacuous by showing a concrete example where it fails when the underlying assumptions are violated. Specifically, the paper should include a synthetic example where the data distributions are not linearly separable in any feature space (e.g., XOR-like patterns or concentric circles) and show that the rate reduction objective cannot achieve high values. This would establish that the objective has bite and isn't trivially maximizable, which is essential for validating the theoretical contribution.

- No worked example for complete network construction: While the paper derives the general form of ReduNet, it never provides a complete worked example showing how to compute all parameters for even a simple 2-layer network on a tiny dataset. The paper should walk through the explicit computation of E₁, C₁^j, and the nonlinear activations for a toy dataset with just 3-4 samples per class, showing how the matrices are constructed and how the forward pass transforms the input. This would help readers understand the practical implementation and verify that the mathematical derivations translate to actual computations.

- Unverified claim about spectral domain efficiency: The paper claims that convolutional networks are more efficient to learn and construct in the spectral domain, but provides no empirical verification of this claim. The paper should include a concrete experiment comparing the computational time and memory usage of implementing ReduNet in the spatial vs spectral domains on the same dataset (e.g., the 1D shift-invariant experiment). This would substantiate the efficiency claim and demonstrate the practical benefit of the spectral approach.

- Missing implementation guidance for multi-channel lifting: The paper introduces multi-channel lifting via filters k_c but provides minimal guidance on how these filters should be chosen in practice. The paper should include a concrete example showing how different choices of filters (e.g., random Gaussian vs Gabor filters) affect the learned representations on a simple dataset, and provide practical recommendations for filter selection. This matters because the lifting operation is critical for the method to work on real data, and without guidance, practitioners cannot effectively apply the method.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive experiments on standard benchmarks (CIFAR-10, ImageNet subset) to validate scalability and performance without backpropagation

- Provide theoretical convergence analysis for the iterative layer construction, including convergence rates and landscape analysis

- Rigorously derive the nonlinear activation functions from the rate reduction objective or justify their optimality

- Include detailed computational complexity analysis and scalability studies with large-scale datasets

- Analyze and relax the strong assumptions about invariance and data structure, demonstrating robustness when assumptions are violated

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about learning without backpropagation
comments.quote: Our preliminary experiments indicate that such a network can already learn a good discriminative deep representation without any back propagation training.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the network can learn 'a good discriminative deep representation' without backpropagation, but this statement is too strong given the limited experimental validation. Based on the paper's own experiments section, this claim is only supported by synthetic Gaussian mixtures and binary MNIST classification (digits 0 and 1). These toy problems don't justify the general claim about learning 'good discriminative deep representations' - the standard in the field would require validation on at least one standard benchmark like full MNIST or CIFAR-10. The abstract should be more precise about the scope of the empirical validation. Rewrite the sentence as 'Our preliminary experiments on synthetic datasets and simple binary classification tasks indicate that such a network can learn discriminative representations without any back propagation training.'

comments.title: Overstated claim about 'precise interpretation' of all components
comments.quote: All components of this “white box” network have precise optimization, statistical, and geometric interpretation.
comments.explanation: This claim overstates the paper's contributions. While the paper derives linear operators from rate reduction principles, the nonlinear components (softmax and sparsity-promoting thresholding) are introduced heuristically rather than derived from first principles. The paper itself acknowledges these are 'motivated rather than rigorously derived.' Therefore, not ALL components have 'precise optimization interpretation.' The claim should be qualified to reflect that linear components are precisely derived while nonlinear components are motivated heuristics. Rewrite as 'The linear components of this "white box" network have precise optimization, statistical, and geometric interpretation, while nonlinear components are motivated by these principles.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated efficiency claim for spectral domain
comments.quote: The derivation also indicates that such a convolutional network is significantly more efficient to learn and construct in the spectral domain.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the derivation shows spectral domain construction is "significantly more efficient" but provides no mathematical analysis or complexity comparison to support this claim. Without showing the computational complexity in both domains or providing a theoretical bound on the efficiency gain, this statement remains an unsubstantiated assertion. The paper should either remove "significantly" or provide concrete complexity analysis demonstrating the claimed efficiency improvement.

comments.title: Undefined membership matrix notation
comments.quote: For convenience, let $\bm{\Pi}^{j}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}$ be a diagonal matrix whose diagonal entries encode the membership of samples/features belong to the $j$-th class: $\bm{\Pi}^{j}(i,i)=\bm{\pi}(\bm{x}^{i})=\bm{\pi}(\bm{z}^{i})$.
comments.explanation: The function $\bm{\pi}(\cdot)$ is undefined. Since $\text{tr}(\bm{\Pi}^{j})$ appears in $\alpha_j$ and $\gamma_j$, this ambiguity affects the objective. Add the explicit definition: $\bm{\pi}(\bm{x}^{i}) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if sample } i \text{ belongs to class } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise}\end{cases}$ because the mathematical formulation requires precise definition of class membership for computing the rate reduction objective.

comments.title: Unjustified WLOG normalization assumption
comments.quote: WLOG, we may assume all features $\bm{z}^{i}$ are normalized to be of unit norm: $\bm{z}^{i}\in\mathbb{S}^{n-1}$.
comments.explanation: The WLOG claim is unjustified since $\Delta R(\bm{Z})$ is not scale-invariant. Consider $\bm{Z}' = c\bm{Z}$ with $c > 0$. Then $\bm{Z}'\bm{Z}'^T = c^2\bm{Z}\bm{Z}^T$, so $R(\bm{Z}') = \frac{1}{2}\log\det(\bm{I} + \alpha c^2\bm{Z}\bm{Z}^T) \neq R(\bm{Z})$. Remove 'WLOG' and state: 'We constrain features to unit norm as a modeling choice to ensure scale invariance in the learned representations' because the normalization constrains the optimization space and is not without loss of generality.

comments.title: Undefined softmax parameter λ
comments.quote: $\widehat{\pi}^{j}(z_{\ell}) \doteq \frac{\exp(-\lambda\|C_{\ell}^{j}z_{\ell}\|)}{\sum_{j=1}^{k}\exp(-\lambda\|C_{\ell}^{j}z_{\ell}\|)} \in [0,1]$ .
comments.explanation: The parameter λ appears without definition or explanation. This parameter controls the sharpness of class membership estimation and is critical for the network's behavior. The paper never discusses how λ should be chosen or its impact on the rate reduction objective. Add after the softmax definition: "The parameter λ > 0 controls the sharpness of the class membership estimation and should be chosen based on the scale of the projection norms ||C_ℓ^j z_ℓ||. In practice, λ can be set as the inverse of the average projection norm across all classes and training samples at layer ℓ."

comments.title: Missing justification for sparsity assumption
comments.quote: One way of resolving this conflict is to leverage additional structure within each class, in the form of sparsity: signals within each class can be assumed to be generated not as arbitrary linear combinations of basis vectors, but as sparse combinations of atoms of different (incoherent) dictionaries  $\mathcal{D}_j$ .
comments.explanation: The paper introduces sparsity as a solution to the subspace separability problem but provides no justification for why this assumption is valid or necessary. The claim that signals within each class can be assumed to be sparse combinations of atoms from incoherent dictionaries is stated without evidence or reference to supporting theory. This is a critical assumption that enables the entire multi-channel approach, yet its validity is not established. Add after the sentence: "This assumption is motivated by empirical observations in natural signal processing where signals often admit sparse representations in appropriate bases (Mallat, 2009), and by theoretical results showing that sparse representations enhance discriminability between classes (Wright et al., 2009)."

comments.title: Unverified convergence claim
comments.quote: This iterative process, once converged say after  $L$  iterations, gives the desired feature map  $f(\pmb{x}, \pmb{\theta})$  on the input  $\pmb{z}_0 = \pmb{x}$ , precisely in the form of a deep network
comments.explanation: The paper claims the iterative process "once converged" but provides no analysis of convergence conditions. The gradient ascent scheme for maximizing rate reduction may not converge for arbitrary initial conditions or parameter choices. There's no discussion of whether the objective is concave, what step size η ensures convergence, or under what conditions the iterations converge to a local maximum. Add after equation (11): "The convergence of this iterative gradient ascent scheme depends on the choice of step size η and the initial features Z_0. Under standard assumptions for gradient ascent with a sufficiently small step size and assuming the rate reduction objective is locally concave around the optimum, the iterations converge to a local maximum. In practice, we observe convergence within L layers for the datasets considered in our experiments."

comments.title: Inconsistent normalization in gradient ascent update
comments.quote:
a > 0$ :

$$
\boldsymbol {Z} _ {\ell + 1} \propto \boldsymbol {Z} _ {\ell} + \eta \cdot \left. \frac {\partial \Delta R}{\partial \boldsymbol {Z}} \right| _ {\boldsymbol {Z} _ {\ell}} \quad \text {s u b j e c t} \quad \boldsymbol {Z} _ {\ell + 1} \subset \mathbb {S} ^ {n - 1}.
comments.explanation: The proportional notation (∝) typically means normalization to unit norm, but it's ambiguous whether this applies column-wise or to the entire matrix. Given that Z_{ℓ+1} ⊂ S^{n-1} means each column should be on the unit sphere, and later in equation (11) the paper uses P_{S^{n-1}} for explicit normalization, the notation in (3) is indeed unclear. Rewrite equation (3) as: $\boldsymbol {Z} _ {\ell + 1} = \mathcal{P}_{\mathbb{S}^{n-1}}[\boldsymbol {Z} _ {\ell} + \eta \cdot \left. \frac {\partial \Delta R}{\partial \boldsymbol {Z}} \right| _ {\boldsymbol {Z} _ {\ell}}]$ where $\mathcal{P}_{\mathbb{S}^{n-1}}$ denotes column-wise normalization to unit sphere, making the normalization operation explicit and consistent with the constraint.

comments.title: Undefined notation in rate reduction formula
comments.quote: Rate Reduction: $\Delta R(\bm{Z})\doteq\underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\log\det\left(\bm{I}+\alpha\bm{Z}\bm{Z}^{*}\right)}_{R(\bm{Z})}-\underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{k}\frac{\gamma_{j}}{2}\log\det\left(\bm{I}+\alpha_{j}\bm{Z}\bm{\Pi}^{j}\bm{Z}^{*}\right)}_{R_{c}(\bm{Z},\bm{\Pi})},$ (1)
comments.explanation: The notation $\bm{Z}^{*}$ is used without definition. Since $\bm{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ and features are real-valued, $\bm{Z}^{*}$ should be explicitly defined as the transpose $\bm{Z}^T$. The identity matrix dimension should also be specified as $\bm{I}_m$ since $\bm{Z}\bm{Z}^T \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$. Rewrite the formula as $\Delta R(\bm{Z})\doteq\frac{1}{2}\log\det\left(\bm{I}_m+\alpha\bm{Z}\bm{Z}^T\right)-\sum_{j=1}^{k}\frac{\gamma_{j}}{2}\log\det\left(\bm{I}_m+\alpha_{j}\bm{Z}\bm{\Pi}^{j}\bm{Z}^T\right)$ because the mathematical rigor requires explicit notation for transpose and matrix dimensions.

comments.title: Parameter sharing inconsistency
comments.quote: $\bm{Z}_{2}=\left[\text{circ}(\bm{x}^{1}+\eta g(\bm{x}^{1},\bm{\theta}_{1})),\ldots,\text{circ}(\bm{x}^{m}+\eta g(\bm{x}^{m},\bm{\theta}_{m}))\right]$
comments.explanation: The notation $\bm{\theta}_{m}$ suggests sample-specific parameters, which contradicts weight sharing in convolutional networks. Earlier in the equation, $g(\cdot,\bm{\theta}_{1})$ uses a single parameter vector. This should be consistent. Rewrite as: '$\bm{Z}_{2}=\left[\text{circ}(\bm{x}^{1}+\eta g(\bm{x}^{1},\bm{\theta}_{1})),\ldots,\text{circ}(\bm{x}^{m}+\eta g(\bm{x}^{m},\bm{\theta}_{1}))\right]$' to maintain parameter sharing across all samples.

comments.title: Notational inconsistency in circulant matrix definition
comments.quote:
\[ \text{circ}(\bar{\bm{z}})\doteq\begin{bmatrix}\text{circ}(\bm{z}[1])\\
\vdots\\
\text{circ}(\bm{\hat{z}}[C])\end{bmatrix}\ \in\mathbb{R}^{nC\times n},\quad\bm{\Sigma}\doteq\begin{bmatrix}\text{circ}(\bm{z}[1])\\
\vdots\\
\text{circ}(\bm{\hat{z}}[C])\end{bmatrix}\left[\,\text{circ}(\bm{z}[1])^{*},\ldots,\text{circ}(\bm{z}[C])^{*}\,\right]\,\in\mathbb{R}^{nC\times nC}, \] (14)
comments.explanation: In equation (14), the first matrix uses $\text{circ}(\bm{\hat{z}}[C])$ while the second matrix uses $\text{circ}(\bm{z}[C])^{*}$. The hat notation $\bm{\hat{z}}$ appears only once and is undefined elsewhere in the text. This creates ambiguity in the definition. The consistent notation should use $\bm{z}[C]$ throughout both matrices. Rewrite equation (14) as: \[ \text{circ}(\bar{\bm{z}})\doteq\begin{bmatrix}\text{circ}(\bm{z}[1])\\ \vdots\\ \text{circ}(\bm{z}[C])\end{bmatrix}\ \in\mathbb{R}^{nC\times n},\quad\bm{\Sigma}\doteq\begin{bmatrix}\text{circ}(\bm{z}[1])\\ \vdots\\ \text{circ}(\bm{z}[C])\end{bmatrix}\left[\,\text{circ}(\bm{z}[1])^{*},\ldots,\text{circ}(\bm{z}[C])^{*}\,\right]\ \in\mathbb{R}^{nC\times nC} \] because the hat notation is undefined and creates inconsistency.

comments.title: Dimensional mismatch in sparse thresholding equation
comments.quote:
$$
\bar {\boldsymbol {z}} = \boldsymbol {\tau} \left[ \operatorname {c i r c} \left(\boldsymbol {k} _ {1}\right) \boldsymbol {z}, \dots , \operatorname {c i r c} \left(\boldsymbol {k} _ {C}\right) \boldsymbol {z} \right] \quad \in \mathbb {R} ^ {n \times C}. \tag {15}
comments.explanation: The notation $\boldsymbol{\tau}[\cdot, \dots, \cdot]$ is ambiguous for how $C$ separate vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ are combined into an $n \times C$ matrix. While the intent is clear (apply thresholding to each convolution result and stack as columns), the mathematical notation is imprecise. Rewrite equation (15) as: $$\bar{\boldsymbol{z}} = \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\tau}(\text{circ}(\boldsymbol{k}_1)\boldsymbol{z}) & \cdots & \boldsymbol{\tau}(\text{circ}(\boldsymbol{k}_C)\boldsymbol{z}) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times C}$$ because this explicitly shows the construction of the $n \times C$ matrix from the $C$ thresholded convolution results.

comments.title: Missing scope qualifier for convolutional networks
comments.quote: This work attempts to interpret modern deep (convolutional) networks from the principles of rate reduction and (shift) invariant classification.
comments.explanation: The abstract suggests the work interprets 'modern deep (convolutional) networks' generally, but the paper's derivation specifically relies on shift invariance as the key symmetry. Modern convolutional networks incorporate many other invariances and architectural features (like pooling, batch normalization, residual connections) that are not addressed. The claim should be scoped to the specific class of networks the theory actually covers. Rewrite as 'This work attempts to interpret a class of deep networks with shift-invariant convolutional layers from the principles of rate reduction and invariant classification.'

comments.title: Ambiguous "good discriminative" performance claim
comments.quote: Our preliminary experiments indicate that such a network can already learn a good discriminative deep representation without any back propagation training.
comments.explanation: The term "good discriminative" is vague and lacks quantitative benchmarks. Without specifying what constitutes "good" performance or providing concrete metrics, readers cannot evaluate the significance of this claim. The abstract should either provide specific performance metrics or qualify this claim more modestly to reflect the preliminary nature of the experiments.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a novel theoretical framework for deriving deep network architectures from the principle of maximal coding rate reduction (MCR²). The key contribution is a white-box network (ReduNet) where all components are explicitly constructed via forward propagation, without backpropagation training. The paper makes strong claims about interpretability and theoretical grounding, but the empirical validation is limited to small-scale synthetic and simple real datasets (MNIST).

The paper offers an elegant theoretical derivation connecting rate reduction to deep network architectures, which is a significant conceptual contribution. The derivation of convolutional structures from shift-invariance is particularly novel. However, the empirical validation is insufficient to support the paper's claims about practical applicability, and several critical assumptions (e.g., tractability of the rate reduction computation, generalization to large-scale problems) are not adequately addressed. The paper would benefit from more rigorous experiments and clearer acknowledgment of limitations.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient empirical validation for claimed practical relevance: The paper claims that ReduNet 'can already learn a good discriminative deep representation without any back propagation training' and suggests potential for scaling to real-world datasets. However, the experiments are limited to: (1) synthetic mixture of Gaussians in 2D/3D, (2) 1D signal classification with 400 training samples, and (3) MNIST digit classification using only 2,000 training samples and a polar coordinate transformation. No results are shown on standard benchmarks like CIFAR-10/100, SVHN, or ImageNet subsets. The paper does not report classification accuracy numbers for any dataset, only visualizations and MCR² loss curves. For a paper claiming to derive practical deep network architectures, the lack of quantitative classification performance on standard benchmarks is a critical omission. The authors should include at minimum classification accuracy on CIFAR-10 or a comparable dataset, with comparisons to standard baselines (e.g., ResNet-20, VGG-16) under similar conditions.

- Computational complexity and scalability concerns unaddressed: The paper acknowledges that computing the rate reduction gradient involves inverting matrices E_ℓ and C_ℓ^j of size n×n and nC×nC respectively. While the spectral domain computation reduces complexity to O(nC³), this is still prohibitive for large n and C. For example, a modest 32×32 RGB image with n=3072 and C=64 would require O(3072×64³) ≈ 800 million operations per layer. The paper's experiments use tiny n (150 for 1D, 1000 for MNIST) and small C (5-7), which are far from practical scales. The paper does not discuss memory requirements, which would be O(nC²) for storing the block-diagonal matrices. A practical deep network would need hundreds of layers and thousands of channels, making this approach infeasible. The authors should provide a detailed complexity analysis and discuss whether the method can scale to modern architectures.

- Unsupported claim about shift-invariance leading to convolutions: The paper claims that when classification is 'rigorously shift-invariant,' the ReduNet naturally becomes a convolutional network. However, the derivation relies on the assumption that the data augmentation includes all circular shifts of each sample, which is only feasible for 1D signals of length n (producing n copies per sample). For 2D images of size W×H, this would require WH copies per sample, which is computationally prohibitive. Moreover, the paper uses circular shifts (toroidal topology), which is not realistic for images where translation invariance should be local, not circular. The paper does not address how to handle boundary effects or how the method would work with natural images where shifts are not circular. The claim that the resulting network is 'rigorously invariant' is misleading because practical implementations would require approximations.

- The sparsity assumption is introduced ad-hoc without justification: In Section 2.3, the paper introduces a sparsity-promoting nonlinear thresholding τ(·) to resolve the conflict between linear subspace modeling and invariance. This is presented as a 'natural remedy,' but it is introduced without any derivation from the rate reduction principle. The paper states that signals within each class can be assumed to be 'sparse combinations of atoms of different (incoherent) dictionaries,' but this assumption is not justified from the MCR² objective. This undermines the paper's claim of deriving all network components from first principles. The authors should either derive the sparsity requirement from the rate reduction objective or acknowledge that this is an additional assumption not implied by the principle.

- The membership estimation function π̂^j is not derived from the principle: In Section 2.2, the paper introduces a softmax-based membership estimator π̂^j(z_ℓ) = exp(-λ||C_ℓ^j z_ℓ||) / Σ_j exp(-λ||C_ℓ^j z_ℓ||) to approximate the unknown class membership π^j(z_ℓ). This function is motivated by geometric intuition but is not derived from the rate reduction objective. The paper does not analyze how errors in this approximation affect the gradient ascent procedure or the convergence of the overall algorithm. Since this nonlinearity is a critical component of the network (it appears in every layer), its ad-hoc nature weakens the claim of a fully principled derivation. The authors should either provide a principled derivation of this nonlinearity or analyze the sensitivity of the network to this choice.

- No comparison to standard deep learning baselines: The paper does not compare ReduNet's performance to standard deep learning architectures (e.g., ResNet, VGG, LeNet) on any dataset. While the paper states that it is 'not the purpose of this work to push the state of the art,' some baseline comparison is necessary to validate that the derived network is competitive with hand-designed architectures. Without such comparison, readers cannot assess whether the rate reduction principle leads to practically useful networks. The authors should include at minimum a comparison to a simple CNN or MLP of comparable size on the MNIST rotation task, reporting classification accuracy.

- The paper overclaims about 'no back propagation training': The paper repeatedly emphasizes that ReduNet is constructed 'without any back propagation training.' However, the construction still requires computing the gradient of the MCR² objective, which involves matrix inversions and multiplications that are computationally expensive. Moreover, the paper does not discuss how the hyperparameters (step size η, precision ε, number of layers L, sparsity threshold) are chosen. In practice, these would likely need to be tuned, which could be considered a form of training. The claim that the network is 'white box' and requires no training is misleading because the construction process itself is a form of optimization that requires significant computation and hyperparameter selection.

- No quantitative classification accuracy on any benchmark: The paper claims the ReduNet 'already gives a good discriminative representation (and achieves good classification performance),' yet reports zero classification accuracy numbers. The experiments show only visualizations of feature geometry and MCR² loss curves on synthetic data, 1D signals, and MNIST with polar coordinates. For a paper that derives a practical deep network architecture, the absence of a simple accuracy table on a standard benchmark like CIFAR-10 or even MNIST (in standard pixel space) is a major gap. The authors should compute and report top-1 classification accuracy on at least one standard dataset, using a nearest-subspace classifier as suggested, and compare to a simple baseline such as a two-layer MLP or a small ResNet.

- Missing worked parametric example for the rate reduction gradient: The paper derives the gradient ascent update (equations 4-6) in full generality but never computes it for a concrete, fully-specified model. A natural worked example would be to take two classes with known Gaussian distributions in ℝ², compute the matrices E_ℓ and C_ℓ^j explicitly in closed form, and verify that the update step (10) indeed separates the classes. This would let readers calibrate their intuition about the role of the covariance matrices and the step size η. Without such an example, the derivation remains abstract and hard to check.

- No demonstration that the convolutional structure is non-vacuous: The paper claims that for shift-invariant data, the ReduNet automatically becomes a convolutional network. However, it does not verify that the resulting convolution kernels are non-trivial or that the network actually outperforms a non-convolutional version. A simple experiment would be to construct ReduNet on 1D shift data with and without enforcing the circulant structure (i.e., using generic matrices versus circulant matrices), and compare the MCR² loss and classification accuracy. If both versions perform similarly, the claim that shift invariance 'naturally leads' to convolutions is weakened. The authors should include this comparison.

- Inference guidance for hyperparameter selection is absent: The paper introduces several hyperparameters — step size η, precision ε, number of layers L, sparsity threshold, and the parameter λ in the softmax membership estimator — but provides no guidance on how to set them. For a paper that claims practical relevance, this omission is notable. The authors should add a brief discussion or a small sensitivity analysis showing how the MCR² loss and classification accuracy vary with η, ε, and L on a simple dataset. At minimum, they should state whether these parameters are chosen by cross-validation or by a fixed rule.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a theoretically interesting framework for deriving deep networks from rate reduction, but the empirical validation is insufficient to support the practical claims. The computational complexity and scalability issues are not adequately addressed, and several key components are introduced ad-hoc rather than derived from the principle. The paper would need substantial additional experiments on standard benchmarks, a complexity analysis, and clearer acknowledgment of limitations to be publishable at a top venue.

Revision targets:

- Add quantitative classification results on at least one standard benchmark (e.g., CIFAR-10, SVHN) with comparison to a simple baseline (e.g., a 3-layer CNN or ResNet-20), reporting accuracy, training time, and model size.

- Provide a detailed computational complexity analysis (time and memory) for the ReduNet construction and inference, including scaling behavior with n, C, L, and m. Discuss whether the method is feasible for modern architectures.

- Derive or justify the sparsity-promoting nonlinearity τ(·) from the rate reduction principle, or explicitly acknowledge it as an additional assumption not implied by MCR².

- Analyze the sensitivity of the network to the choice of hyperparameters (η, ε, L, λ) and provide guidance on how to set them without extensive tuning.

- Include a discussion of limitations: circular shift assumption, boundary effects for images, scalability to large datasets, and the ad-hoc nature of the membership estimator.

comments.title: Missing classification accuracy numbers
comments.quote: It is not the purpose of this work to push the state of the art on any real datasets with highly engineered networks and systems, although we believe this framework has this potential in the future.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that ReduNet 'achieves good classification performance' (abstract) and 'learns a good discriminative representation,' yet reports zero classification accuracy numbers. For the MNIST rotation experiment, only heatmaps and loss curves are shown. Without quantitative accuracy, the claim of 'good classification performance' is unsubstantiated.
